title: The Startup Owner's Manual
subtitle: The Step-by-Step Guide for Building a Great Company
author: Steve Blank

coauthor.name: Bob Dorf
knownFor: Co-author of The Startup Owner's Manual

themes: Startups search for a business model; companies execute one
Customer Development as an alternative to Product Development
Get out of the building — customer interaction is the only source of truth
Hypothesis-driven experimentation replaces rigid business plans
Iterative pivoting based on validated learning
Premature scaling is the #1 killer of startups
Earlyvangelists are the only early customers that matter
awards: Required reading for NSF I-Corps program
Taught at Stanford, Berkeley, Columbia and 100+ universities
Catalyst of the Lean Startup movement
excerpt: The definitive playbook for building a scalable startup using Customer Development. Replace guesswork with validated learning: get out of the building, test hypotheses, find product-market fit, then scale.
keyIdeas: Startups search for a business model; companies execute one. The distinction changes everything about how you operate.
Customer Development replaces the traditional Product Development model with four iterative steps: Discovery, Validation, Creation, Building.
Get out of the building. There are no facts inside your building — only opinions. Customer interaction is the only source of truth.
The Business Model Canvas is the organizing framework for startup hypotheses. State every assumption before testing it.
Minimum Viable Product is the smallest feature set that gets earlyvangelists to pay. Build only what you need to test.
Pivot or proceed: every Customer Development cycle ends with a data-driven decision. Pivoting is not failure — it is course correction.
Premature scaling is the #1 killer of funded startups. Validate before you build a sales force.
Earlyvangelists are the only customers that matter in the early stage: they have the problem, know they have it, and are actively seeking a solution.
keyTakeaways: A startup is a temporary organization in search of a repeatable and scalable business model — not a small version of a big company.
The Customer Development Manifesto has 14 rules including: there are no facts inside the building, pair Customer Development with Agile development, and failure is an integral part of the search.
Customer Discovery has four phases: state hypotheses, test the problem, test the solution, verify the business model.
Customer Validation has four phases: get ready to sell, get out and sell, position and refine, pivot or proceed.
The nine deadly sins of startups include: assuming you know customer needs, focusing on a launch date instead of learning, and premature scaling.
Metrics that matter for startups are actionable, accessible, and auditable — not vanity metrics like total registered users.
Physical channel and web/mobile channel startups require different tactics within the same Customer Development framework.
whoShouldRead: First-time founders building a scalable startup
Entrepreneurs who raised money and are struggling to find product-market fit
Product managers transitioning from corporate to startup environments
Accelerator teams looking for a structured process
University entrepreneurship students (Stanford, Berkeley, Columbia use it)
NSF Innovation Corps teams (the book is required reading for the program)
whoShouldSkip: Lifestyle business owners who do not plan to scale
Readers who want inspirational memoirs (try Shoe Dog or Losing My Virginity)
Corporate managers looking for innovation playbooks (read The Lean Startup instead)
Anyone expecting a quick read — this is a 608-page reference manual
relatedBooks: The Four Steps to the Epiphany — Steve Blank
The Hard Thing About Hard Things — Ben Horowitz
Venture Deals — Brad Feld & Jason Mendelson
